summary:
Oracle bone script is the earliest confirmed form of Chinese writing, used during the [[event:55|Shang dynasty]] roughly between 1250 and 1046 BC. It was inscribed on the shoulder blades of cattle and the undershells of tortoises, which were then subjected to intense heat until cracks formed. Shang diviners interpreted the pattern of these cracks as answers from royal ancestors and deities to questions about warfare, agriculture, weather, royal health, and ritual propriety. The questions and often their interpreted answers were then incised onto the bone or shell surface, creating a record of the divination.

Oracle bones were used primarily at the Shang capital [[event:1841|Yin Xu]] near modern Anyang in Henan province, where enormous caches of inscribed bones have been excavated since their rediscovery in the late nineteenth century. Over 150,000 inscribed oracle bone fragments are known, containing thousands of distinct characters. Scholars deciphered the script beginning in the 1890s and established that these characters are direct ancestors of modern Chinese writing, with many individual characters recognizable in the script used today after thousands of years of evolution.

The oracle bone inscriptions provide the most direct textual evidence for Shang dynasty history, recording the names of Shang kings, the structure of the royal court, religious practices, and the geographic extent of Shang political reach. The content of the divinations reveals a society deeply concerned with maintaining proper relationships with royal ancestors through ritual sacrifice, and one in which divination was a royal prerogative central to governance. The oracle bone corpus remains one of the most important bodies of ancient texts in any language.
